heading: Public record; examination of records by Comptroller General; exemptions: exceptional conditions; reports to Congress
label: 1433 Public record; examination of records by Comptroller General; exemptions: exceptional conditions; reports to Congress
notes: Amendments1966—Subsec. (b). provided for exemption of certain contracts with foreign contractors from the requirement for an examination-of-records clause, such determination to be reported to Congress.Pub. L. 89–607

Nonapplicability of the National Emergencies ActThe provisions of the National Emergencies Act [see Short Title note set out under ] shall not apply to the powers and authorities conferred by this section and actions taken hereunder, see .section 1601 of this titlesection 1651(a)(4) of this title

Exemption of FunctionsFunctions with respect to purchases authorized to be made outside the limits of the United States or the District of Columbia under the Foreign Assistance Act of 1961, as amended [see Short Title note set out under , Foreign Relations and Intercourse], as exempt, see Ex. Ord. No. 11223, eff. , 30 F.R. 6635, set out under .section 2151 of Title 22section 2393 of Title 22May 12, 1965

Foreign ContractorsSecretaries of Defense, Army, Navy, or Air Force, or their designees, to determine, prior to exercising the authority provided in the amendment by to exempt certain contracts with foreign contractors from the requirement of an examination-of-records clause, that all reasonable efforts have been made to include such examination-of-records clause, as required by par. 11 of Part I of Ex. Ord. No. 10789, and that alternate sources of supply are not reasonably available, see par. 11 of Part I of Ex. Ord. No. 10789, eff. , 23 F.R. 8897, as amended, set out under .Pub. L. 89–607section 1431 of this titleNov. 14, 1958

children.label: a
children.text: All actions under the authority of this chapter shall be made a matter of public record under regulations prescribed by the President and when deemed by him not to be detrimental to the national security.

where the contractor or subcontractor is a foreign government or agency thereof or is precluded by the laws of the country involved from making its books, documents, papers, or records available for examination; and

where the agency head determines, after taking into account the price and availability of the property or services from United States sources, that the public interest would be best served by the omission of the clause.
